# The same machinery, in software

## What `primarycensored` solves

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
- Really a **wrapper** for any delay distribution: it adds the primary
  and secondary censoring and truncation around whatever family you
  choose
- **Analytic** primary-censored CDFs for gamma, lognormal, and Weibull
  with a uniform primary; numerical integration otherwise
- Signed support (normal, logistic, Laplace, Cauchy, Gumbel) for
  delays that can be negative
- And a **non-parametric, hazard-based** estimator
  ([#312](https://github.com/epinowcast/primarycensored/pull/312)) — it
  needs an eye on it, and is not clearly a good idea: for onward reuse
  we have little guidance yet on how to estimate or report these
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.callout-note}
One likelihood, many delays, with the bias adjustments handled for
you.
:::

::: {.attribution}
[primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) ·
non-parametric estimator:
[primarycensored#312](https://github.com/epinowcast/primarycensored/pull/312)
:::
:::
::::

## Point-source outbreaks are the same problem

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- A common-source outbreak shares one exposure window
- The distribution of exposure times within the source may itself
  differ — it need not be uniform
- Estimate the incubation period from onset dates; the same censoring
  and dynamical-bias issues apply
- A Legionnaires' model in use by UKHSA does this, deconvolving onsets
  into a release time and an incubation period
  ([Egan & Hall 2011](https://doi.org/10.1097/EDE.0b013e31820937c6))
- It extends naturally to **multiple candidate sources**
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-pointsource.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk, after the point-source incubation idea of
[Egan & Hall (2011)](https://doi.org/10.1097/EDE.0b013e31820937c6)
:::
:::
::::

## Tied into the ecosystem

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- The same delay $f$ enters three of our tools in different forms:
  - **`epidist`** — individual-level delays from line lists
  - **`EpiNow2`** — delays in `estimate_dist`, `estimate_truncation`,
    `estimate_infections`
  - **`epinowcast`** — population-level nowcasting via reporting
    **hazards**, with uncertainty on the counts
- How to choose between them is an open question — is there guidance in
  the time-to-event literature?
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
`epidist` — individual likelihood
$$ \textstyle\prod_i f(t_i) $$

`EpiNow2` — convolve incidence
$$ C_t = \sum_{\tau} f(\tau)\, I_{t-\tau} $$

`epinowcast` — reporting hazards on counts
$$ \mathbb{E}[n_{t,d}] = \lambda_t\, p_d $$
$$ p_d = h_d\textstyle\prod_{d'<d}(1-h_{d'}) $$

::: {.attribution}
[epidist](https://epidist.epinowcast.org/) ·
[EpiNow2](https://epiforecasts.io/EpiNow2/) ·
[epinowcast](https://package.epinowcast.org/)
:::
:::
::::

## What is coming: more windows, more families

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
- The censored CDF is the delay density $f$ convolved with the
  primary-event window $H_w$:

$$ F_{\mathrm{cens}}(q) = \int_0^q H_w(q-u)\, f(u)\, du $$

- Expanding the window as exponentials reduces this to **Laplace-type
  transforms** $T_f(\xi;\tau)=\int_0^\tau e^{\xi u} f(u)\,du$ of $f$:

$$ F_{\mathrm{cens}}(q) = \sum_k a_k(q)\, T_f(\xi_k; q) $$

- The **window** (uniform, exponentially tilted, logistic, Gumbel)
  sets the $a_k, \xi_k$; the **delay family** sets whether $T_f$ is
  analytic
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
| Delay family | Transform |
|---|---|
| exponential, gamma, inverse Gaussian | analytic |
| Weibull, generalised gamma, Gompertz, log-logistic, Burr | semi-analytic |
| lognormal | numerical |

::: {.attribution}
Transform extension to non-uniform windows (working paper); logistic
and Gumbel windows after
[Egan & Hall (2011)](https://doi.org/10.1097/EDE.0b013e31820937c6)
:::
:::
::::
